questions.question: Data loss or data exfiltration is best defined as:
questions.option_a: A. The accidental deletion of files from a local hard drive only
questions.option_b: B. When data is intentionally or unintentionally lost, stolen, or leaked to the outside world
questions.option_c: C. The authorised transfer of data between two trusted systems
questions.option_d: D. The corruption of data caused solely by hardware failures
questions.explanation: Data loss or data exfiltration occurs when data is lost, stolen, or leaked to unauthorised parties, whether intentionally or accidentally.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered an example of organisational data that could be lost or stolen?
questions.option_a: A. Employee personal social media posts
questions.option_b: B. Publicly available news articles
questions.option_c: C. Research and development data
questions.option_d: D. Marketing billboards and public advertisements
questions.explanation: Organisational data includes R&D data, sales, financial, HR, legal, employee, contractor, and customer data — representing valuable organisational assets.

questions.question: Which of the following is a consequence of data loss for an organisation?
questions.option_a: A. Increased employee productivity
questions.option_b: B. Improved customer loyalty and trust
questions.option_c: C. Reduced operating costs
questions.option_d: D. Litigation and legal action resulting in fines and civil penalties
questions.explanation: Data loss can lead to brand damage, loss of competitive advantage, customer loss, revenue loss, and legal action with fines and civil penalties.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a vector of data loss?
questions.option_a: A. Unencrypted devices
questions.option_b: B. Removable media
questions.option_c: C. Hard copy documents
questions.option_d: D. Intrusion detection systems
questions.explanation: Vectors of data loss include unencrypted devices, cloud storage, removable media, hard copy, improper access control, email, and social networking. Intrusion detection systems are security controls.

questions.question: Improper Access Control is listed as a vector of data loss. What does this mean?
questions.option_a: A. Employees accessing the internet during work hours for personal use
questions.option_b: B. Sensitive data being accessible to people who should not have permission to view it
questions.option_c: C. The use of personal email accounts for work communications
questions.option_d: D. The failure to update antivirus software regularly
questions.explanation: Improper access control means sensitive organisational data may be accessed by unauthorised individuals due to weak or misconfigured access mechanisms.

questions.question: BYOD stands for:
questions.option_a: A. Bring Your Own Data
questions.option_b: B. Build Your Own Device
questions.option_c: C. Bring Your Own Device
questions.option_d: D. Business Yield on Demand
questions.explanation: BYOD stands for Bring Your Own Device, referring to employees using personal devices to access organisational networks for work.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the BYOD concept?
questions.option_a: A. A policy that prohibits employees from using any personal devices at work
questions.option_b: B. A business model where the company provides employees with company-owned mobile devices
questions.option_c: C. The trend of employees using personal devices to remotely access organisational networks for work
questions.option_d: D. A cloud computing strategy for storing corporate data on employee-owned servers
questions.explanation: BYOD allows employees to use personal devices like smartphones, tablets, and laptops to access organisational networks remotely for work purposes.

questions.question: Which of the following is a benefit of BYOD?
questions.option_a: A. It eliminates all security risks associated with mobile devices
questions.option_b: B. It transfers full responsibility for IT support to employees
questions.option_c: C. It boosts productivity as employees can access work on their personal devices anytime
questions.option_d: D. It gives the IT department complete control over all personal devices
questions.explanation: BYOD boosts productivity because employees can access work anytime on their personal devices, using devices they are comfortable with.

questions.question: Which of the following is a financial benefit of BYOD, particularly for small and medium businesses (SMBs)?
questions.option_a: A. SMBs receive government subsidies for implementing BYOD
questions.option_b: B. BYOD eliminates the need for any IT department in SMBs
questions.option_c: C. The money saved on buying hardware and software can be utilised for other things
questions.option_d: D. SMBs can charge employees for using the company network on personal devices
questions.explanation: BYOD saves money on purchasing hardware and software, allowing SMBs to invest in other areas instead.

questions.question: Which of the following is a drawback of BYOD in an organisation?
questions.option_a: A. Employees become less productive when using their own devices
questions.option_b: B. Companies cannot legally access company data on personal devices
questions.option_c: C. BYOD devices may get stolen or lost, causing data breaches
questions.option_d: D. BYOD always leads to higher hardware procurement costs for companies
questions.explanation: BYOD devices can be lost or stolen, potentially causing data breaches and increasing IT security management challenges.

questions.question: What is the main security threat arising from BYOD?
questions.option_a: A. Employees deliberately leaking data via personal emails only
questions.option_b: B. Malware entering the corporate network through improperly secured personal devices
questions.option_c: C. Personal devices automatically syncing all corporate data to public clouds
questions.option_d: D. The inability to install antivirus software on personal devices
questions.explanation: The main BYOD security threat is malware entering the corporate network via personal devices that are not properly secured.

questions.question: COPE stands for:
questions.option_a: A. Corporate-Owned, Personally Enabled
questions.option_b: B. Company-Operated, Personally Employed
questions.option_c: C. Corporate-Only, Privately Encrypted
questions.option_d: D. Centrally Operated, Personally Equipped
questions.explanation: COPE stands for Corporate-Owned, Personally Enabled, allowing employees to use company-provided devices as if they were personal.

questions.question: How does the COPE model differ from BYOD?
questions.option_a: A. In COPE, employees purchase their own devices and get reimbursed by the company
questions.option_b: B. In COPE, the organisation provides the devices to employees but allows personal use, giving the company greater control
questions.option_c: C. In COPE, employees are not allowed to use devices for any personal activity
questions.option_d: D. COPE and BYOD are identical models with different names
questions.explanation: COPE provides company devices for employees with personal use allowed, giving the organisation more control over data and security.

questions.question: Which of the following is a benefit of COPE over BYOD?
questions.option_a: A. Employees can install any software they wish without restriction
questions.option_b: B. The organisation can remotely wipe corporate data if an employee loses their device or leaves the organisation
questions.option_c: C. The company bears no responsibility for data breaches on COPE devices
questions.option_d: D. COPE devices are cheaper to procure than BYOD devices
questions.explanation: COPE allows organisations to remotely wipe corporate data if devices are lost or employees leave, enhancing security control.

questions.question: What does MDM stand for in the context of BYOD/COPE security?
questions.option_a: A. Mobile Data Monitoring
questions.option_b: B. Managed Device Model
questions.option_c: C. Mobile Device Management
questions.option_d: D. Multi-Device Mechanism
questions.explanation: MDM stands for Mobile Device Management, which is used to secure, monitor, and manage mobile devices in corporate environments.

questions.question: Which of the following is a Mobile Device Management (MDM) security feature?
questions.option_a: A. Allowing employees to install any app without approval
questions.option_b: B. Disabling all network access for personal devices
questions.option_c: C. Jailbreak and root detection on managed devices
questions.option_d: D. Automatically transferring corporate data to personal cloud accounts
questions.explanation: MDM security features include data encryption, PIN enforcement, remote data wipe, and jailbreak/root detection to secure devices.

questions.question: An employee's corporate-issued phone is stolen. Which MDM feature would BEST protect the organisation's data on that device?
questions.option_a: A. PIN enforcement
questions.option_b: B. Jailbreak detection
questions.option_c: C. Remote data wipe of the stolen device
questions.option_d: D. Data Loss Prevention options
questions.explanation: Remote data wipe allows an organisation to erase all corporate data from a lost or stolen device, preventing unauthorised access.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the definitions of a 'hacker' as described in the lecture?
questions.option_a: A. A clever programmer capable of developing new programs to make them more efficient
questions.option_b: B. A network professional who uses sophisticated skills to ensure networks are not vulnerable
questions.option_c: C. A government employee who legally monitors network traffic for national security
questions.option_d: D. A person who tries to gain unauthorized access to devices on the Internet
questions.explanation: A government employee monitoring traffic legally is not considered a hacker. Hacker definitions include clever programmers, network security professionals, unauthorised access seekers, and those running disruptive programs.

questions.question: White Hat Hackers are best described as:
questions.option_a: A. Criminals who hack for financial gain using any means necessary
questions.option_b: B. Government-sponsored attackers who steal intelligence and sabotage systems
questions.option_c: C. Ethical hackers who use their skills for good, legal purposes and report vulnerabilities to developers
questions.option_d: D. Inexperienced hackers who run pre-built scripts made by others
questions.explanation: White Hat Hackers are ethical hackers who identify and report vulnerabilities to improve security legally and responsibly.

questions.question: A security researcher discovers a critical vulnerability in a widely-used application without permission, and then publicly announces it before the developer can patch it. This person is best categorised as a:
questions.option_a: A. White Hat Hacker
questions.option_b: B. Gray Hat Hacker
questions.option_c: C. Black Hat Hacker
questions.option_d: D. Script Kiddie
questions.explanation: Gray Hat Hackers exploit systems without permission but not for personal gain or to cause damage, sometimes publicly disclosing vulnerabilities.

questions.question: Black Hat Hackers are distinguished from other hacker types primarily by:
questions.option_a: A. Their focus on improving network security through legal means
questions.option_b: B. Their tendency to report vulnerabilities to vendors for rewards
questions.option_c: C. Their use of only pre-existing tools and scripts written by others
questions.option_d: D. Being unethical criminals who violate security for personal gain or malicious reasons
questions.explanation: Black Hat Hackers violate security laws and exploit vulnerabilities for personal gain or malicious purposes.

questions.question: Script Kiddies are best described as:
questions.option_a: A. Highly skilled hackers who develop advanced zero-day exploits for profit
questions.option_b: B. Government-funded hackers who create custom attack tools to steal intelligence
questions.option_c: C. Inexperienced hackers who run existing scripts and exploits developed by others, typically not for profit
questions.option_d: D. Ethical hackers who perform security assessments for organisations
questions.explanation: Script Kiddies are inexperienced hackers who use existing scripts and tools to cause harm, often for recognition rather than profit.

questions.question: Vulnerability Brokers are typically associated with which type of hacker?
questions.option_a: A. Black Hat Hackers — they sell exploits on the dark web to the highest bidder
questions.option_b: B. White Hat Hackers — they are employed by companies to find and fix vulnerabilities
questions.option_c: C. Gray Hat Hackers — they discover exploits and report them to vendors, sometimes for a reward
questions.option_d: D. State-Sponsored Hackers — they trade exploits between intelligence agencies
questions.explanation: Vulnerability Brokers are usually gray hat hackers who discover vulnerabilities and report them for a reward, aiming to improve security.

questions.question: Cyber Criminals operate in an underground economy. Which of the following items would they typically buy, sell, or trade?
questions.option_a: A. Cybersecurity certifications and training courses
questions.option_b: B. Attack toolkits, zero-day exploit code, botnet services, banking Trojans, and keyloggers
questions.option_c: C. Legitimate software licences and antivirus subscriptions
questions.option_d: D. Government cybersecurity policy documents
questions.explanation: Cyber Criminals trade malicious tools, exploits, and stolen information in an underground economy.

questions.question: Hacktivists differ from Cyber Criminals primarily because:
questions.option_a: A. Hacktivists only target government systems while cyber criminals target businesses
questions.option_b: B. Hacktivists use more sophisticated tools than cyber criminals
questions.option_c: C. Hacktivists hack for attention and to protest social/political ideas, not for financial profit
questions.option_d: D. Hacktivists always act within the law while cyber criminals do not
questions.explanation: Hacktivists attack systems to protest or raise awareness, not for financial gain, unlike cyber criminals.

questions.question: The Stuxnet malware, which was created to damage Iran's nuclear enrichment capabilities, is an example of an attack by which type of threat actor?
questions.option_a: A. Script Kiddies
questions.option_b: B. Cyber Criminals
questions.option_c: C. Hacktivists
questions.option_d: D. State-Sponsored Hackers
questions.explanation: Stuxnet is a State-Sponsored attack, created by government-funded hackers targeting critical infrastructure with custom malware.
